coredata.dc:title: Detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on f–f transition of Eu2+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xuan
Liu, Litao
Zhang, Wei
Na, Liyan
Hua, Ruinian
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we designed Eu2+-doped BaAlF5 and BaSiF6 downconversion nanophosphors with strong f–f transition emission to realize the quantitative detection of TNP. The surface of the as-prepared nanophosphors was modified by polyethylenimine (PEI) to increase the water solubility and to provide a pair of electrons to bind to the benzene ring in TNP, forming a Meisenheimer complex. The detection principle of TNP is based on luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) technology, where the PEI-functionalized BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors are used as energy donors and TNP is an energy acceptor. With increasing TNP concentrations, the luminescence intensity of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors at 359 nm [4f7(6P7/2)→4f7(8S7/2) transition of Eu2+] displays dramatic luminescent quenching. The BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI or BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphor downconversion luminescent sensors show great sensitivity and selectivity and good linearity, and the detection limits of TNP are 0.57 and 2.82 ng/mL, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BaAlF5:Eu2+
BaSiF6:Eu2+
f–f transition of Eu2+
LRET
TNP
Rare earths

Luminescence spectra of the as-prepared products
FT-IR and the absorption spectra of the samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the BaSiF6:8 mol% Eu2+@PEI (1), BaSiF6:8 mol%Eu2+ (2), BaAlF5:8 mol% Eu2+@PEI (3) and BaAlF5:8 mol%Eu2+ (4) samples.
SEM images of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ (a), BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI (b), BaSiF6:Eu2+ (c) and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI (d) nanophosphors.
Luminescence spectra of BaAlF5:x mol%Eu2+ (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) nanophosphors (a) and BaSiF6:x mol%Eu2+ (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) nanophosphors (b).

(a) FT-IR spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ (1), BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI (2), BaSiF6:Eu2+ (3) and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI (4) nanophosphors; (b) The emission spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors and the absorption UV-vis absorption of TNP.
Emission spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ (a) and BaSiF6:Eu2+ (b) nanophosphors before and after PEI capping.

(a) Luminescence spectra of BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors with different concentrations of TNP; (b) The curve of luminescence intensity of BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors at 359 nm after adding different concentrations of TNP; (c) The linear relationship between the luminescence intensity of the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors and the TNP concentration (1–5 ng/mL).

(a) Luminescence spectra of BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors with different concentrations of TNP; (b) The linear relationship between the luminescence intensity of the BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanoparticles at 359 nm and different concentrations of TNP.

Emission spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors and the absorption spectra of interfering substances (dibutyl-phthalate, trimesic aid, methylbenzene, p-anisidine, benzene) (a). Emission spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI (b) and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI (c) nanophosphors in the presence of different interfering substances (dibutyl-phthalate, trimesic aid, methylbenzene, p-anisidine, benzene).
Emission spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI (a) and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI (b) nanophosphors with different concentrations of alcohol.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Foundation item: Project supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21173034) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (wd01206).

head.ce:title: Detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on f–f transition of Eu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Dalian Nationalities University, Dalian 116600, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Dalian Nationalities University, Dalian 116600, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Microelectronics, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Microelectronics, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we designed Eu2+-doped BaAlF5 and BaSiF6 downconversion nanophosphors with strong f–f transition emission to realize the quantitative detection of TNP. The surface of the as-prepared nanophosphors was modified by polyethylenimine (PEI) to increase the water solubility and to provide a pair of electrons to bind to the benzene ring in TNP, forming a Meisenheimer complex. The detection principle of TNP is based on luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) technology, where the PEI-functionalized BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors are used as energy donors and TNP is an energy acceptor. With increasing TNP concentrations, the luminescence intensity of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors at 359 nm [4f7(6P7/2)→4f7(8S7/2) transition of Eu2+] displays dramatic luminescent quenching. The BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI or BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphor downconversion luminescent sensors show great sensitivity and selectivity and good linearity, and the detection limits of TNP are 0.57 and 2.82 ng/mL, respectively.

The luminescent spectra of BaAlF5:Eu2+ nanophosphors are decreased with increasing the concentration of TNP (1–15 ng/mL) at 359 nm (6P7/2-8S7/2) and present a good linear relationship between the concentration of TNP and luminescent intensity. It proves that the TNP can be detected via luminescence quenching of BaAlF5:Eu2+ nanophosphors by using a LRET system. The luminescent intensity of nanophosphors after adding different concentrations of TNP via Siskowski experiential equation can be fitted well. The curve is
y = 1.27 ×
10 5
− 3.7338 ×
10 4
× ln
( 1 + c / 9.88 )
, R 2 = 0.99, the luminescent intensity of BaAlF5:Eu2+ nanophosphors and TNP concentrations (1–5 ng/mL) show a good linear relationship, and the detection limit of the TNP is 0.57 ng/mL.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para:
Nitroaromatics, including 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), nitrobenzene (NB), nitrotoluene (NT) and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), are important chemicals that are used widely in the production of various products, such as explosives, pesticides, herbicides, dyes, and pharmaceuticals. 1–3 Moreover, with the development of the economy and the chemical engineering industry, demand for nitroaromatics is increasing. However, nitroaromatics such as TNP or TNT have been classified as hazardous chemicals because of their proven toxic effects. 4 Environmental problems, such as soil and groundwater pollution, occur when these compounds are overused and released into the environment. 5–7 More importantly, nitroaromatics also have explosive properties, and they will cause not only casualties and property losses but also social panic. TNP, one of the nitroaromatics, has recently been a specific concern, threatening to cause adverse health impacts on human beings, such as aplastic anemia, cancer, disturbances of liver function, or discoloration of skin and hair. 8
,
9 Although TNP can be beneficial to our lives, unreasonable overuse will cause a series of problems. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a fast and efficient method for monitoring the trace levels of TNP.

At present, various analytical methods, such as mass spectrometry, 10 surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), 11 high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), 12 and gas chromatography-isotope ratio mass spectrometry (GC-IRMS), 13 have been employed for the detection of TNP. Nakashima et al. 14 reported the detection of TNP by mass spectrometry, and the limit of detection (LOD) was approximately 54 × 10−12 mol/L. Zhang et al. 15 found that TNP could be detected by SERS, with a LOD of up to 10−5 mol/L. Moore and Sommadossi 16 found a method for the detection of TNP by HPLC, and the LOD was approximately 0.63 ng/mL. However, most of these traditional techniques have high cost and low sensitivity, and the detection process was time consuming.

In recent years, various luminescent materials with the advantages of low cost and high sensitivity have been developed to detect TNP, such as organic molecules, 17–19 rare-earth-doped nanoparticles, 20 metal complexes, 21
,
22 coordination polymers (CPs), 23–25 semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), 26–28 and metal-organic framework (MOF) compounds. 29–31 The detection of TNP by luminescent materials was mostly based on LRET. LRET is a long-range nonradiative process through dipole-dipole interactions, where an excited-state donor (usually a fluorophore) can transfer energy to a proximal ground-state acceptor (the analyte). 32 Kaur et al. 26 found that the detection of TNP can be realized by nitrogen-doped graphene QDs via LRET, and the LOD was 4.2 × 10−7 mol/L. Rong et al. 20 studied graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets as LRET donors to detect TNP, and the LOD was approximately 8.2 × 10−9 mol/L. The primary disadvantages of these QD materials are their low sensitivity and ease of oxidation and bleaching. Joarder et al. 29 reported that the detection of TNP could be actualized by an aqueous-phase MOF, with a LOD of up to 1.29 × 10−6 mol/L. The synthesis process for MOFs is complicated, and MOFs are easily influenced by pH in the environment, making MOFs unstable and difficult to preserve. Compared with QDs and MOFs, rare earth-doped nanomaterials are easier to be prepared and have good stability and luminescence properties. Wang et al. 33 found that the detection of TNP can be achieved by guanidine-functionalized upconversion luminescent nanoparticles (UCNPs@GDN) via LERT, and the LOD was approximately 7.78 × 10−13 mol/L. Ma et al. 34 found that a UCNP (NaYF4@PSI-NH) nanosensor can be used to detect TNP and TNT via LRET and obtained LODs of approximately 9.6 ng/mL and 8.4 ng/mL, respectively.

Currently, because rare-earth-doped UCNPs have many advantages, they are widely used to detect nitroaromatics with high sensitivity, and the LOD can reach ng/mL level. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is currently no report on the detection of nitroaromatics via LRET technology based on rare earth-doped downconversion nanophosphors. Therefore, in this work, rare earth ion (Eu2+)-doped BaAlF5 and BaSiF6 nanophosphors were synthesized by a hydrothermal technique and then modified by PEI to increase their water solubility. The luminescence intensity was transferred from the electron-rich PEI-capped nanophosphors to the electron-deficient benzene ring of TNP, which caused luminescence quenching. The BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphor downconversion luminescent sensors show great sensitivity and selectivity, with good linearity between the luminescence intensity and TNP concentration.

Europium(II) dichloride and branched polyethylenimine (PEI) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Crystalline AlCl3·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, Ba(OH)2·8H2O, NH4HF2, HF, H2SiO3 solution, dibutyl-phthalate, trimesic aid, methylbenzene, p-anisidine and benzene were obtained from Beijing Chemical Corporation (China). All the chemical reagents were analytical grade and were used without further purification.

BaAlF5:Eu2+ nanophosphors were prepared through our previously reported procedures. 35 First, (2–c) mmol of BaCl2·2H2O, 2 mmol of AlCl3·6H2O, c mmol of EuCl2 (c=0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10) and 10 mL of deionized (DI) water were mixed and then magnetically stirred until the mixed solution was clear. Then, an NH4HF2 (5 mL, 0.7 mol/L) solution was instilled slowly into the mixed solution. After magnetically stirring for 20 min, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated (200 °C, 24 h). The precipitates were separated by centrifugation and washed with DI water at room temperature. Later, the BaAlF5:x mol% Eu2+ (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) nanophosphors were obtained after drying in air at 80 °C.

BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors were prepared through our previously reported procedures. 36 First, (2–c) mmol of Ba(OH)2·8H2O, c mmol of EuCl2 (c = 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10), 4 mmol of H2SiO3 and 4 mmol of NH4HF2 were mixed with 30 mL of DI water, and the pH value (3–4) of the mixed solution was adjusted by HF under magnetic stirring until the chemicals were dissolved completely. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated (180 °C, 72 h). Later, the precipitates were separated by centrifugation, washed with DI water and dried in air at 80 °C. Eventually, BaSiF6:x mol% Eu2+ (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) nanophosphors were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of nanophosphors with PEI
ce:section.ce:para: BaAlF5:8 mol%Eu2+ nanophosphors (0.2 g) and PEI (2 g) were mixed with 10 mL of DI water under magnetic stirring for 8 h. Then, the nanophosphors were collected by centrifugation, washed with DI water and dried in air. Subsequently, BaAlF5:8 mol%Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors were obtained. 37 The BaSiF6:8 mol%Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors were obtained by the same way.

The structure of all nanophosphors was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, XRD-6000, Shimadzu). The scanning rate was 2.0 (°)/min and the 2θ range was from 10° to 50°. The size and morphology of the nanophosphors were determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800). The fluorescence spectra were obtained by an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared nanophosphors are shown in Fig. 1 . All peaks of BaAlF5:8 mol%Eu2+ and BaAlF5:8 mol%Eu2+@PEI match well with the BaAlF5 standard data (JCPDS card No.36-1193). Furthermore, all peaks of the BaSiF6:8 mol%Eu2+ and BaSiF6:8 mol%Eu2+@PEI samples can also be indexed well with the orthorhombic BaSiF6 crystals (JCPDS card No. 84-0628). No other impurity diffraction peaks were observed, indicating that pure orthorhombic-phase BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ can be prepared by convenient hydrothermal technique.

The morphologies of the nanophosphors are shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that BaAlF5:Eu2+ and PEI-modified BaAlF5:Eu2+ nanophosphors exhibit rice particle morphologies with a uniform size of 40 nm in diameter. After modification by PEI, the morphology and particle size of PEI-modified BaAlF5:Eu2+ nanophosphors did not change obviously compared with those of BaAlF5:Eu2+. The morphologies of BaSiF6:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI were uniform nanorods, and the sizes were approximately 200–800 nm in length and 30 nm in diameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence spectra of the as-prepared products
ce:section.ce:para: Under the same measurement conditions of excitation at 278 nm, the emitting peak intensities of the BaAlF5:x mol%Eu2+ (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) and BaSiF6:x mol%Eu2+ (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) nanophosphors at 359 nm are shown in Fig. 3 . The strongest emission peak of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors is located at 359 nm, and this peak is identified as the 4f7 (6P7/2) →4f7(8S7/2) transition of Eu2+ in the range from 340 to 400 nm. When the Eu2+ doping concentration increases from 2 mol% to 8 mol%, the luminescence intensity of the f–f transition increases; subsequently, the luminescence intensity decreases when the doping concentration of Eu2+ is more than 8 mol%. Therefore, the BaAlF5:8 mol%Eu2+ and BaSiF6:8 mol%Eu2+ nanophosphors were chosen for surface modification and used for quantitative analysis of TNP samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and the absorption spectra of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify that PEI was modified on the surface of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors, the FT-IR spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors were measured. Fig. 4 (a) (2, 4) show a special characteristic peak in the region at approximately 1650 cm−1, which was attributed to the amine group (–NH2) bending vibration. 38 The band at 3450 nm was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the –OH and –NH2 groups. These results show that PEI was successfully modified on the surface of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors. It can be seen clearly from Fig. 4(b) that the UV-visible absorption spectrum of TNP overlaps well with the emission spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors at 359 nm. Furthermore, the electron-deficient TNP group and the electron-rich amine group in PEI formed a Meisenheimer complex by the charge-transfer complexing interaction, and the distance between the TNP and PEI-capped nanophosphors was less than ten nanometers. Thus, it could constitute an LRET system to detect TNP, in which the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors were used as energy donors and TNP served as the acceptor. In addition, the emission spectra of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors were obtained before and after PEI modification (as shown in Fig. 5 ). It can be seen from the emission spectra that the spectral shape was almost unchanged, indicating that the optical properties of the BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors were not affected by PEI.

The BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors (5 mg) in DI water (4 mL) were added to the experimental bottle and magnetically stirred for 4 h. Then, the emission spectra of the nanophosphor solution mixed with X μL (X = 10, 20, …, 140, 150, n = 15) of TNP (400 ng/mL) were measured. Fig. 6 (a) shows the luminescence spectra of BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors with different concentrations of TNP. The luminescence intensity at 359 nm (6P7/2-8S7/2) decreased with increasing TNP concentration, and good linearity between the TNP concentration and the luminescence intensity was observed, as shown in Fig. 6(b). This proves that TNP can be detected via luminescence quenching of BaAlF5:Eu2+ nanophosphors by using an LRET system.

We need to establish a model to describe the luminescence quenching phenomenon of nanophosphors. As everyone knows, surface tension will change after a solution is mixed with a different solvent. When a TNP alcohol solution was added to the aqueous nanophosphor solution, the surface tension of the solution gradually decreased, leading to the slight aggregation of the nanophosphors. Hence, the amino groups on the surface of the nanophosphors were reduced, which led to a decrease in the binding ratio between the amino groups and TNP. When TNP reaches a certain degree, no amino groups bind with TNP. Therefore, energy resonance transfer will not occur, and quenching will not occur.

The relationship between the luminescence intensity of BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors and the TNP concentration can be written as Eq. (1), which is similar to the Siskowski experiential equation:
(1)
y =
I ο
×
[
δ o
−
δ o
× b × In
(
1 +
c a
)
]
where I 0 is the initial luminescence intensity of BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors, δ 0 is the interfacial tension of water (72.1), and a and b are two uncertain parameters of an experimental equation that was employed to express the relationship of surface tension and solution concentration.

From Fig. 6(b), it can be seen that the luminescence intensity of nanophosphors and the TNP concentration can be fitted well via Eq. (1). The curve is
y = 1.27 ×
10 5
− 3.7338 ×
10 4
× i n
( 1 + c / 9.88 )
, R 2 = 0.99. From Fig. 6(c), the luminescence intensity of BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors and the TNP concentrations (1–5 ng/mL) show a good linear relationship. The limit of quantification was 1 ng/mL, and the theoretical detection limit calculated via 3δ/S was 0.57 ng/mL. To verify that the method of detecting TNP via LRET technology based on the f–f transition of Eu2+ was practicable, another BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphor was used as an energy donor to detect TNP via LRET technology. The emission spectra of the BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphor solution with a series of concentrations of TNP (X = 30, 40, 60, 80, …,180, 200, n = 10) were measured. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . The curve is
y = 1.05 ×
10 5
− 1.7535 ×
10 9
× l n
( 1 + c / 6.6 ×
10 6
)
, R 2 = 0.993. The luminescence intensity of BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors and the TNP concentrations (3–20 ng/mL) also showed a good linear relationship. The limit of quantification was 3 ng/mL, and the theoretical detection limit calculated via 3δ/S was 2.82 ng/mL. Thus, it was demonstrated that the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors modified with –NH2 groups as energy donors to detect TNP via LRET technology were practicable.

It is known that selectivity is the key for detection sensors, and it is necessary to verify the selectivity of the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphor sensors for TNP. Therefore, interfering substances (dibutyl-phthalate, trimesic aid, methylbenzene, p-anisidine, benzene, 400 ng/mL, 30 μL), which are similar to TNP in terms of chemical properties, were used. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the luminescence spectra and absorption spectra of the nanophosphors and interfering substances were obtained. The characteristic absorption band of the interfering substances did not overlap with the emission band of the nanophosphors, and there was no energy transfer. Hence, the luminescence spectra of the nanophosphors were not significantly changed in the presence of different interfering substances (shown in Fig. 8(b) and (c)). To avoid the ethanol effect, blank experiments were performed. A pure ethanol solution was added to the solution of nanophosphors. It can be seen from Fig. 9 that the luminescence spectra were almost unchanged with increasing ethanol concentration. The results demonstrate that ethanol has no effect on the luminescence intensity of the nanophosphors. This investigation reveals that the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphor sensors are selective for TNP.

In summary, downconversion BaAlF5:Eu2+ and BaSiF6:Eu2+ nanophosphors were synthesized by a hydrothermal method and modified by PEI on the surface to obtain hydrophilic nanoparticles. In the LRET system, the hydrophilic nanophosphors were used as energy donors to detect TNP, where the energy acceptor can absorb the f-f transition energy of Eu2+ at 359 nm, causing quenching of the luminescence intensity of BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors. There is a linear relationship between the luminescence intensity of the nanophosphors and the concentration of TNP, and the detection limits are 0.57 and 2.82 ng/mL for the BaAlF5:Eu2+@PEI and BaSiF6:Eu2+@PEI nanophosphors, respectively.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Moore JD, Sommadossi JP. Determination of O-(chloroacetylcarbamoyl)fumagillol (TNP-470; AGM-1470) and two metabolites in plasma by high-performance liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry with atmospheric pressure chemical ionization. Biological Mass Spec. 1995;30:1707.
